summary: Gruber argues that Apple's refusal to support Flash on iPhone OS (and the upcoming tablet) is driven by both engineering pragmatism and corporate strategy. He details how Flash is the leading cause of application crashes on Mac OS X according to Apple's own Crash Reporter data, and how Flash's 32-bit-only status forced Apple to engineer an entirely new plugin architecture for Safari. He contends that Flash's status as the only de facto web standard controlled by a single proprietary company is harmful to the open web, and that Apple is solving this by pushing H.264 and HTML5 as open alternatives. He acknowledges Adobe's claim that Apple doesn't expose the APIs needed for hardware-accelerated video on Mac, but argues this reflects Apple's deliberate engineering philosophy of controlling the full stack. Ultimately, he concludes that the iPhone's lack of Flash hasn't hurt Apple at all, and that Apple's stance is both principled and self-interested.
key_points: Flash is the single largest source of application crashes on Mac OS X according to Apple's Crash Reporter data, and its 32-bit-only status forced Apple to build an entirely new plugin architecture for Safari

Flash is uniquely problematic as the only de facto web standard based on proprietary technology — Apple is pushing H.264 and HTML5 as open replacements, not substituting its own proprietary alternative
Apple's chicken-and-egg strategy is working: the iPhone created the first large audience without Flash, forcing publishers to adopt open video standards
Adobe claims Apple won't expose hardware acceleration APIs for Flash video on Mac, but this reflects Apple's philosophy of controlling the abstraction layer between software and hardware
Apple's anti-Flash stance is simultaneously principled engineering (controlling the full source stack) and political strategy (weakening a competitor's platform dominance)
money_quotes: The single leading source of application crashes on Mac OS X is a component that Apple can't fix.
Flash is the only de facto web standard based on a proprietary technology.
The best argument against Flash support is that it is harmful to the web as a whole to have something as important as video be in the hands of a single company.
Apple isn't trying to replace Flash with its own proprietary thing. They're replacing it with H.264 and HTML5. This is good for everyone but Adobe.
Apple, with the iPhone, is solving the chicken and egg problem. For the first time ever, there is a large and growing audience of demographically desirable users who don't have Flash installed.
The iPhone's lack of Flash has not hurt it one bit.
Apple's distaste for Flash Player is both a matter of engineering taste and politics.
We believe that we need to own and control the primary technologies behind the products we make, and participate only in markets where we can make a significant contribution.
key_insight: Apple's rejection of Flash is both an engineering conviction about controlling the full technology stack and a strategic bet that open web standards will replace proprietary plugins — and the iPhone proves the strategy is working.
